questions.questionText: Under Section 375 IPC, rape is defined as sexual intercourse with a woman:
questions.optionB: Against her will, without consent, by coercion/fraud, when she cannot give valid consent (intoxicated, unsound mind, or age <18 years)
questions.explanation: Section 375 IPC (now BNS Sec 63): defines rape — sexual intercourse with a woman: against her will, without consent, consent obtained by fear/fraud/deception, when she is unable to communicate consent, intoxicated/drugged, of unsound mind, or UNDER 18 years (statutory rape — regardless of consent). Punishment: Sec 376 IPC — minimum 10 years RI to life imprisonment. Section 377 IPC: unnatural sexual offences. POCSO Act 2012: sexual offences against children (<18). Reference: Reddy — Sexual Offences.

questions.questionText: In a case of alleged rape, the most important forensic evidence is:
questions.optionB: Detection of spermatozoa/seminal fluid (acid phosphatase, Florence test, Barberio test) and DNA profiling
questions.explanation: Forensic examination for sexual assault: (1) Spermatozoa detection: gold standard (vaginal swab — motile sperm survive up to 72 hrs). (2) Seminal fluid tests: acid phosphatase (screening — elevated in semen), Florence test (choline crystals), Barberio test (spermine crystals). (3) DNA profiling: STR analysis from semen stains — most definitive for identification. (4) Injuries: genital (hymenal tears, vaginal injuries) and extragenital. (5) Clothing, pubic hair, foreign material. Two-finger test: NOT recommended (SC ruling). Reference: Reddy — Examination of Rape Victim.

questions.questionText: Virginity can be conclusively proven by:
questions.optionB: No medical examination can conclusively prove or disprove virginity (hymen may be absent congenitally, torn by non-sexual activity, or intact after intercourse)
questions.explanation: Virginity: CANNOT be conclusively proven or disproven by any medical examination. Hymen: highly variable in morphology; may be absent congenitally, torn by exercise/tampon use, or remain intact after intercourse (distensible hymen). Supreme Court of India: two-finger test is unconstitutional and violates right to privacy. Intact hymen does NOT prove virginity. Torn hymen does NOT prove sexual intercourse. 'True knot' in hymen: old tear with healed edges. Reference: Reddy — Virginity.
